coredata.dc:title: Efficient one-pot synthesis of molecularly imprinted silica nanospheres embedded carbon dots for fluorescent dopamine optosensing
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2012 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Mao, Yan
Bao, Yu
Han, Dongxue
Li, Fenghua
Niu, Li
coredata.dc:description: A new type of eco-friendly molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) was synthesized through an efficient one-pot room-temperature sol–gel polymerization and applied as a molecular recognition element to construct dopamine (DA) fluorescence (FL) optosensor. Highly luminescent carbon dots (CDs) were firstly synthesized via a one-step reaction in organosilane, and their surface were anchored with MIP matrix (CDs@MIP). The resulting composite of a synergetic combination of CDs with MIP showed high photostability and template selectivity. Moreover, the composite allowed a highly sensitive determination of DA via FL intensity decreasing when removal of the original templates. The new MIP-based DA sensing protocol was applied to detect DA concentration in aqueous solution, the relative FL intensity of CDs@MIP decreased linearly with the increasing DA in the concentration range of 25–500nM with a detection limit (3σ) of 1.7nM. Furthermore, the proposed method was successfully intended for the determination of trace DA in human urine samples without the interference of other molecules and ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecularly imprinted polymer
Sol–gel polymerization
Carbon dots
Fluorescence sensor
Dopamine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Template extraction and adsorption of CDs@MIP

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) HRTEM image of CDs; (B) Size distribution histogram of the CDs obtained from the DLS measurement in ethanol; (C) FTIR spectra of AEAPMS and CDs; (D) Absorption and PL emission spectra of the CDs in ethanol (recorded from 340nm to 480nm in 20nm increments).

TEM images of CDs@MIP (A) and CDs@NIP (B); UV–vis absorption (a), fluorescence excitation (b), and emission (c) spectra of the CDs@MIP. Inset is the enlarged UV–vis graph from 230nm to 350nm (C); Stablility of fluorescence emission property of CDs@MIP (50μgmL−1) in water (D).

(A) FTIR spectra of CDs@NIP (c), CDs@MIP before (a) and after (b) template molecules extraction; (B) fluorescence emission spectra of CDs@NIP (a), CDs@MIP before (b) and after (c) template molecules extraction, CDs@MIP (50μgmL−1) with addition of 500nM DA (d); (C) Selective adsorption of DA, AA, and EP by CDs@MIP.

Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs@MIP (A) and CDs@NIP (B) (50μgmL−1) with addition of the indicated concentrations of DA. Insets are the DA concentration calibration curves, error bars=±standard deviation.
Illustration of the preparation of CDs@MIP.
Results of determination of DA in urine samples.

head.ce:title: Efficient one-pot synthesis of molecularly imprinted silica nanospheres embedded carbon dots for fluorescent dopamine optosensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, c/o Engineering Laboratory for Modern Analytical Techniques, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Graduate University of the Chinese Academy of Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun 130022, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new type of eco-friendly molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) was synthesized through an efficient one-pot room-temperature sol–gel polymerization and applied as a molecular recognition element to construct dopamine (DA) fluorescence (FL) optosensor. Highly luminescent carbon dots (CDs) were firstly synthesized via a one-step reaction in organosilane, and their surface were anchored with MIP matrix (CDs@MIP). The resulting composite of a synergetic combination of CDs with MIP showed high photostability and template selectivity. Moreover, the composite allowed a highly sensitive determination of DA via FL intensity decreasing when removal of the original templates. The new MIP-based DA sensing protocol was applied to detect DA concentration in aqueous solution, the relative FL intensity of CDs@MIP decreased linearly with the increasing DA in the concentration range of 25–500nM with a detection limit (3σ) of 1.7nM. Furthermore, the proposed method was successfully intended for the determination of trace DA in human urine samples without the interference of other molecules and ions.

► An eco-friendly CDs@MIP composite was synthesized. ► The synthesis process was a one-pot room-temperature sol–gel polymerization. ► CDs@MIP was the molecular recognition element of fluorescence optosensor. ► The optosensor exhibited high sensitive and selective towards dopamine.

ce:section.ce:para: The molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) technique, a synthetically efficient method towards polymers with molecular recognition properties, has been demonstrated as one of the most promising techniques in sensor field because of its simplicity, reliability, capability of miniaturization, and low cost method of fabrication (Chen et al., 2011; Haupt and Mosbach, 2000). Multifarious methods have been explored to prepare MIP, such as bulk polymerization (Ye et al., 1998), multi-step swelling polymerization (Haginaka et al., 1999), suspension polymerization (Mayes and Mosbach, 1996), and precipitation polymerization (Tamayo et al., 2003), etc. By comparison, the sol–gel polymerization (Makote and Collinson, 1998) exhibits three distinct advantages that: (i) the ease fabrication with thin films and/or bulk gels; (ii) the eco-friendly reaction solvent (water or ethanol), which is quite different from the general solvent (chloroform, acetonitrile or toluene) that used in the above mentioned polymerization methods; (iii) the mild polymerization conditions that allow specific reagents to be readily introduced within the highly cross-linked, porous host structure without the problems of thermal or chemical decomposition.

Dopamine (DA) is an important neurotransmitter in mammalian central nervous systems, detection and quantification of DA are important in diagnoses, monitoring, prevention and treatments of several neurological disorders, e.g., Schizophrenia, Huntington's disease, and Parkinson's disease (Ali et al., 2007). Different analytical methods have been reported for DA determination, such as fluorometry (Huang et al., 2012), visible spectrophotometry (Abbaspour et al., 2009), chromatography combined with mass spectrometry (LC/MS) (Ji et al., 2008), and electrochemical techniques (Shang et al., 2009). Their detection limits are 0.14μM, 3.0μM, 33pM, and 5nM, respectively. Although LC/MC method can reach very low detection limit, the determination requires sample pretreatment, lengthy analysis times, and high costs. In addition, there are a number of problems with electrochemical methods due to the nature of the oxidative electrode reaction of DA. The concentration of DA in the extracellular fluid of the central nervous system is 0.01∼1μM compared to 100–500μM for ascorbic acid (AA, undergo oxidation within the same potential window as DA). Therefore, fluorescence technique is a good choice to combine with MIP technique, which converts the binding events on MIP to physically detectable signals. The combination can achieve both good sensitivity and excellent selectivity.

Photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) constitute a fascinating class of recently discovered nanocarbons owing to their attractive attributes, including tunable photoluminescence (PL) properties, eco-friendliness, low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility (Baker and Baker, 2010). CDs form attractive application as labels for bioimaging (Cao et al., 2007; Yang et al., 2009), but they are rarely involved in the sensor field at present (Gonçalves et al., 2010). The main obstacles are encompassed by immobilization of CDs in a suitable matrix that remains all CDs’ PL properties, permeates to the analytes, and avoids the leaching of CDs.

It should be pointed out that II–IV semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) coated with molecularly imprinted silica film through the sol–gel polymerization approach have been previously reported (Li et al., 2010; Liu et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2009a, b). The fabricated optical sensors could selectively and cooperatively bind to the template molecules. Nevertheless, in order to improve the interaction between QDs and molecularly imprinted silica matrix, QDs have to be capped with organosilane before the sol–gel hydrolysis and condensation imprinted polymerization reaction. It means that the polymer preparation process needs at least two steps.

In this work, we introduce a process with which CDs can be encapsulated with silica imprinted film through a one-pot sol–gel reaction. Furthermore, the PL properties of CDs are maintained since silica is a transparent and inert material (Yang and Gao, 2005). The silica imprinted film here can not only avoid CDs leaching from the matrix, but also show excellent permeability to the template molecules. In the experiments, multi-aminosilane was used as a coordinating solvent to synthesize highly luminescent CDs functionalized with methyldimethoxy silane groups (Wang et al., 2011). And silica molecular imprinted nanospheres could be fabricated in a simple and efficient way from CDs, template molecules (DA), functional monomer (3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane, APTES), and cross-linker (tetraethoxysilane, TEOS) by means of the hydrolysis and condensation reaction in the presence of aqueous ammonia solution as the catalysis (Li et al., 2010). Therefore, a new type of eco-friendly MIP-based DA fluorescence optosensor has been developed by anchoring the MIP matrix on the surface of CDs via an efficient one-pot room-temperature sol–gel molecular imprinting process.

N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropyl methyldimethoxy silane (AEAPMS), TEOS, APTES, citric acid anhydrous were purchased from Alfa Aesar. Dopamine hydrochloride (DA) and epinephrine (EP) were obtained from Sigma. Ascorbic acid (AA) was purchased from Fluka. Ammonia solution (25% in water), petroleum ether, and ethanol were obtained from Beijing Chemicals, China. All aqueous solution were prepared with ultrapure water (>18MΩcm) obtained from a Milli-Q Plus system (Millipore).

Fluorescence spectra were recorded using an F-4600 Hitachi spectrometer. UV–vis spectra were measured on a U-3900 Hitachi spectrometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a Hitachi-600 transmission electron microscope operating at 100kV. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image was recorded with a Philips TF-F20 transmission electron microscope operating at 200kV. Because the AEAPMS-passivated CDs tend to hydrolyze and self-condense to a gel catalyzed by heat and/or trace water from air during the drying process, the HRTEM sample was prepared on a copper grid by dropping 10μL of the CDs ethanol solution onto the mesh and drying it in room-temperature. All Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopic measurements were performed on a Bruker Vertex 70 spectrometer. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was recorded on a Malvern Nano-ZS instrument.

CDs were synthesized from citric acid anhydrous following a method reported by Wang et al. (2011). 10mL AEAPMS were placed into a 100mL three-necked flask, and degassed with nitrogen for about 10min. Then, the system was heated to 240°C, 0.5g citric acid anhydrous was quickly added to the flask with vigorous stirring. The mixture was then kept at the temperature for 1min. After natural cooling to room-temperature, the final products were purified by precipitation with petroleum ether three times. The synthesized CDs were dispersed in a certain amount of ethanol for the next experiments.

DA molecules are used as the template, and the procedure for CDs@MIP preparation is described in Scheme 1. To a 50mL flask, 100mg DA were added to about 35mL CDs ethanol solution, and then used ultrasound to assist DA dissolution. To the resultant mixture, 310μL APTES (functional monomer) and 1.4mL TEOS (cross-linker) were added with stirring. Since DA molecules are easily oxidized in the case of air and light, the reaction should proceed in the nitrogen atmosphere and avoid light. Finally, the mixture of 120μL ammonia solution and 860μL water was added to the reaction flask, and the reaction was allowed to proceed for 24h at room temperature. The resulting product was collected by centrifugation and washed thoroughly with ethanol to discard the reagents. Original DA templates in the imprinted nanoparticles were extracted with ethanol and water, until no template could be detected from the washing solvent with UV spectrometer. The polymer particles were dried in a vacuum chamber at 60°C overnight. As a control, non-imprinted polymer microspheres (CDs@NIP) were prepared following the same procedure, except that no template was added.

The fluorescence measurements were carried out with the excitation wavelength of 360nm. After removal of the templates, the recognition capacity of CDs@MIP (50μgmL−1 in water) was measured with different DA concentrations by fluorescence spectroscopy. The control experiments of CDs@NIP were the same as CDs@MIP.

Human urine samples were collected from healthy laboratory volunteers. All samples were subjected to a 100-fold dilution before analysis, and no other pretreatments were necessary. A quantity of the DA was added to the diluted urine and the resulting urine solutions were used for fluorescent analysis. The content of the DA was determined by the standard addition method.

The preparation of CDs was illustrated in Scheme 1. Details of fabrication could be found in the experiment section. As shown in Scheme 1A, methyldimethoxy silane groups functionalized CDs with highly luminescent property were synthesized via a one-pot reaction. The synthesized highly luminescent organosilane-functionalized CDs were characterized with HRTEM, DLS, FTIR spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The characteristic evidences were demonstrated in Fig. 1. The morphology of CDs was studied by HRTEM and shown in Fig. 1A. It was observed that the CDs exhibited as uniform, mono-dispersed, microspherical particles with an average diameter of 1.5nm. DLS analysis in Fig. 1B demonstrated that the average particle size of the CDs in ethanol is 1.36nm, which was well consistent with the HRTEM result. The basic procedure to synthesize the above mentioned CDs involved decomposition and pyrolysis of anhydrous citric acid, accompanying by the formation of gas and condensable vapors. Correspondingly, the surface passivation reaction of the amine groups in AEAPMS with the carboxyl groups driven from the pyrolysis species occurred simultaneously (Wang et al., 2011). The acylation reaction of the surface passivation process could be confirmed by FTIR spectroscopy. Different from pure AEAPMS, the FTIR spectrum of CDs showed two characteristic peaks at 1650cm−1 and 1565cm−1 assigning to the vibrations of amide I and amide II, respectively (shown in Fig. 1C). The two typical signals, arising from C=ONR vibrations, suggested the successful surface passivation reaction of CDs. As demonstrated in Fig. 1D, the synthesized CDs exhibited an obvious adsorption peak at 360nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum. When excited in the range of 340–400nm, the CDs showed strong blue photoluminescence in the PL emission spectra and their emission were highly dependent on the excitation energy (as the excitation wavelength increased, the emission peak position shifted to longer wavelength and the fluorescence intensity decreased).

The hydrolysis reaction took place on the surface of CDs with the catalysis of aqueous ammonia, which was depicted in Scheme 1B. The resultant CDs further interacted with DA through the hydrogen bond interactions and van der Waals forces. These interactions successfully drove the template molecules into the formed silica matrix. The CDs@MIP was simply fabricated through the hydrolysis and condensation reaction of APTES and TEOS catalyzed by aqueous ammonia solution (Li et al., 2010). After removal of the template molecules by solvent elution, the CDs@MIP with specific DA recognition sites was obtained (shown in Scheme 1A).

The morphology of CDs@MIP and CDs@NIP were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2A and B, the resulting CDs@MIP and CDs@NIP particles showed similar appearances, and both of them exhibited spherical nanostructure with ca. 50nm diameter and relatively narrow size distributions.

Typical UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs@MIP was shown in Fig. 2C(a). The characteristic absorption peak around 250–300nm was enlarged in the inset, which was corresponding to the aromatic π system belonging to CDs. And it was considered similar to that of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (Li et al., 2011). The prepared CDs@MIP showed a symmetric fluorescence emission peak at 450nm when excited at 360nm (Fig. 2C(c)), which was in accordance with the CDs described above. These results suggested that the silica coating has not restricted the PL properties of CDs since the silica is optically transparent and inert (Sun et al., 2008). The relative standard deviation (R.S.D.) of ∼3.95% was obtained by 12 further repeated detections of the fluorescence intensity in the 50μgmL−1 CDs@MIP aqueous solution for every 5min. The results shown in Fig. 2D indicated the stable emission property of the obtained CDs@MIP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Template extraction and adsorption of CDs@MIP
ce:section.ce:para: The conventional solvent elution method was used for template extraction. FTIR spectra of CDs@MIP and CDs@NIP have been compared in Fig. 3A, which further confirmed the successful imprinting of DA into the silica matrix. As shown in Fig. 3A(a), the characteristic peak at 1496cm−1 can be ascribed to the aromatic ring stretching vibration of DA molecule (Pande et al., 2009). After solvent elution, this characteristic peak disappeared in the FTIR spectrum of CDs@MIP (Fig. 3A(b)). It suggested that DA molecules have been successfully imprinted into the silica matrix, and effectively washed out during the solvent extraction process. The strong and broad peak around 1060cm−1 was assigned to the Si–O–Si asymmetric stretching, and the characteristic peaks at 793cm−1 and 455cm−1 were attributed to the Si–O vibrations. All these bonds further confirmed that the imprinted polymer films, which generated from sol–gel condensation of the silane reagents, have successfully grafted on the surface of CDs.

The eco-friendly fluorescent CDs@MIP material can be further applied to efficiently DA sensing. As shown in Fig. 3B(a) and (c), after template extraction, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@MIP nearly reached that of CDs@NIP. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@MIP quenched a lot when interacted with DA molecules, and a charge transfer between CDs and DA molecules was suggested to be responsible for the fluorescence quenching of the CDs@MIP. Wang et al. (2009a, b) demonstrated that the photoluminescence in CDs could be quenched efficiently by electron acceptor or donor molecules in solution. In this recognition system, the CDs and DA molecules acted as electron acceptor and electron donor, respectively. Therefore, the photoluminescence of CDs@MIP might be effectively quenched in case of interacting with DA molecules.

In order to illustrate the selectivity and sensitivity of CDs@MIP towards the template molecule DA, control experiments concerning AA and EP were carried out. Since AA always largely coexists with DA in brain, while EP has the similar structure with DA. Fig. 3C showed that the fluorescence responses of CDs@MIP towards DA were much larger than the other two analogues, which indicated a better adsorption and binding capacity for the template molecules. And the fluorescence quenching degree increased with increment of target molecule concentration. Nonspecific adsorption was intended as a contribution to the fluorescence response of AA and EP. The high specificity of CDs@MIP was probably owing to the shapes of the cavities in the imprinted polymers just fitting for the unique molecular structure of DA. As a result, other analogues cannot be bound tightly into the imprinted cavities. Parallel experiments about DA, AA and EP quenched fluorescence intensity of CDs@NIP were also made. The three analogues nearly quenched the fluorescence of CDs@NIP with the same efficiency, and the quenching efficiency of CDs@NIP was obvious lower than CDs@MIP (data not shown). Which further supported the specific recognition of CDs@MIP was attributed to MIP binding. As a result, CDs@MIP might serve as candidate for applications as molecular detection sensor resulting from their selectivity against other analogues.

The fluorescence intensity of the CDs@MIP turned out to decrease sensitively in the presence of DA molecule. The MIP matrix was used to provide specific binding sites that interacted with the template molecule DA via hydrogen bonds and van der Wall forces. Fig. 4 revealed the effect of DA concentration on the PL intensity of both CDs@MIP (A) and CDs@NIP (B). As reported in the previous literatures, the analyte quenched the QDs chemosensor depending on its concertation, which accorded with the Stern–Volmer equation (Chen and Rosenzweig, 2002). Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of CDs@MIP and CDs@NIP towards DA can also be quantified by a Stern–Volmer type equation:
F
0
/ F = 1 +
K
sv
C
DA
where F 0 and F are the PL intensities in the absence and presence of a quencher (DA is the quencher in this study), respectively, K sv is the quenching constant of the quencher, and C DA is the concentration of DA. As shown in Fig. 4, the CDs@MIP exhibited about 3 times fluorescence quenching efficiency (F 0/F−1) than that of CDs@NIP, which might derive from the efficient imprinting effect. In other words, the recognition sites on the imprinted nanospheres have better steric matching ability with the template molecules, so more template molecules have been allowed to adsorb on the molecular imprinted matrix. Inset of Fig. 4A illustrated the corresponding plot which showed a linear relation between fluorescence response and DA concentration in the range of 25–500nM with a correlation coefficient of 0.999. And the detection limit was estimated to be 1.7nM, which was calculated according to the 3σ criteria.

The developed CDs@MIP fluorescent biosensor was employed for selective and sensitive detection of DA in biological fluids such as human urine samples. The recovery was evaluated by comparing the analytic signals of DA obtained from the spiked urine with those of the same concentration standard solution (n=3, average of three replicate measurements). An appropriate (100-fold) dilution of urine was found sufficient to obtain a quantitative recovery (97.97–102.26%) of spiked DA and to use a simple aqueous standard solution for the accurate quantification of DA ( Table 1).

In summary, a simple one-pot room-temperature synthesis method for the fabrication of molecular recognition sites on the silica nanospheres embedded CDs was developed. The synthesized CDs@MIP was employed as a novel and high selectivity fluorescence probe for optical recognition of DA. Moreover, this strategy can be further expected to fabricate various eco-friendly and selective sensors for advanced applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are most grateful to the NSFC (No. 21175130 and No. 21105096) and Chinese Academy of Sciences (No.KGCX2-YW-231, YZ200906 and YZ2010018) for their financial supports.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Journal of the American Chemical Society
The Journal of Physical Chemistry C
Journal of the American Chemical Society
